sketch: VaultCore exposes one `Gallery` actor per opened gallery, created by an unlock call that runs Argon2id and unwraps the DEK; the actor is the only type holding the DEK and the only code path allowed to write under `galleries/{id}/`. Every mutation (importFile, addTombstone, updateHEAD) is an async actor method: it stages new encrypted chunks and a candidate manifest object into a `wal/{txid}/` directory, fsyncs, then makes the whole batch visible with a single atomic rename plus HEAD pointer swap — a crash before the rename leaves only garbage in `wal/` that startup recovery deletes, and a crash after leaves a fully consistent vault, so no partial import is ever observable. Each successful mutation returns a new immutable `Manifest` value struct (entries, chunk lists, per-file chunk-size, plaintext-BLAKE2b dedup index), and the actor publishes it via an `AsyncStream<Manifest>` so the grid UI, the AVAssetResourceLoader delegate, and a future sync engine each hold their own snapshot generation. Reads never enter the actor: a snapshot plus a Sendable `ChunkReader` (holding a copy of the DEK, or a decrypt-closure capability handed out by the actor) resolves (fileID, byteRange) → chunk range → decrypt-with-derived-nonce entirely off-actor, so N concurrent video scrubs and thumbnail decodes proceed in parallel with zero contention while imports serialize behind them. Because manifest objects are themselves content-addressed and immutable, a snapshot stays valid forever — an in-flight playback keeps streaming from the manifest it opened even if HEAD has since advanced, and the future CRDT sync engine becomes just another actor client: it merges a remote manifest into a candidate, submits it as one more serialized commit, and everyone downstream sees it as an ordinary new snapshot. The same design ports to the macOS/Linux CLI peer unchanged since it is pure Swift Concurrency plus POSIX rename semantics.
risk: The snapshot boundary leaks key material out of the actor: for reads to bypass the actor, the DEK (or an equivalent decrypt capability) must live in Sendable reader values scattered across UI, playback, and sync tasks — which collides head-on with the hardening requirement to zero the DEK on lock/backgrounding. Lock-on-background becomes a distributed invalidation problem (outstanding readers may still hold the key), and if it's solved by routing decrypts back through the actor instead, the whole parallel-read advantage evaporates and video scrubbing serializes behind imports.
first_step: Define the value-type contract before any I/O: create `Sources/VaultCore/Manifest.swift` with immutable `Manifest`, `FileEntry`, and `ChunkRef` structs (all Sendable, Codable, per-file chunkSize), plus a `GalleryActor` skeleton whose only mutation entry point is `func commit(_ tx: (ManifestBuilder) throws -> Void) async throws -> Manifest`, and write a compile-time test that `Manifest` is Sendable and a unit test that two concurrent `commit` calls produce strictly ordered, monotonically advancing snapshots over a temp directory with WAL-rename semantics stubbed in.
children: Revocable ChunkReader capability: the actor hands out reader tokens wrapping the DEK behind an internal generation counter; lock/backgrounding bumps the generation and zeroes the shared key buffer, so stale readers fail closed with a typed VaultLocked error — resolving the load-bearing risk while keeping reads off-actor.

WAL-as-sync-outbox: since every mutation already stages a self-contained chunk+manifest batch in wal/{txid}/ before commit, reuse the same directory format as the upload unit for cloud/peer sync — a committed txid is exactly the set of objects the sync engine must push, giving resumable upload for free.

Snapshot pinning for playback: the AVAssetResourceLoader delegate retains its opening Manifest snapshot for the lifetime of the AVAsset, and a future GC pass treats 'snapshot pinned by live reader' as a root — turning the tombstone/garbage-collection problem into simple refcounting of snapshot generations.

Time-travel debugging and integrity audit: because HEAD history is a chain of content-addressed immutable manifests, keep the last N manifest hashes in a small log; a CLI `vault log` / `vault checkout <hash>` can open any historical snapshot read-only, doubling as a tamper-evidence audit trail before CRDT sync exists.

Deterministic actor replay tests: model GalleryActor commands as a Codable enum and record them during property-based tests, then replay the same command sequence against a fresh temp vault and assert byte-identical final manifest hash — a portable conformance suite the macOS/Linux CLI peer can run to prove the on-disk contract holds across platforms.
